question_text: Which of the following international sporting events is specifically organised for athletes with physical, visual and intellectual disabilities, and is held every four years following the Olympic Games in the same host city?

options.text: The Asian Games, which are open to all athletes regardless of disability status
The Paralympic Games, which feature athletes with physical, visual and intellectual disabilities competing in adapted versions of Olympic sports and Paralympic-specific sports
The FIFA World Cup, which is exclusively for football teams from all nations
The Commonwealth Games, which are restricted to athletes from Commonwealth nations only

explanation: The Paralympic Games are the premier international multi-sport event for athletes with disabilities. Held every four years in the same city as the Olympics (since 1988 Summer, 1992 Winter), they feature athletes with physical, visual and intellectual disabilities competing in adapted Olympic sports and Paralympic-specific sports like boccia and goalball.
elimination_hints: A, C and D are mainstream events not exclusively for athletes with disabilities. Only the Paralympics (B) is specifically organised for differently-abled athletes.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Which of the following correctly explains the difference between the Paralympic Games and the Special Olympics in terms of the target athlete population and competition structure?

options.text: There is no difference; both events serve exactly the same athlete population and use identical formats

The Paralympics feature elite athletes with physical, visual and intellectual disabilities competing at the highest level using classification systems, while the Special Olympics focus on athletes with intellectual disabilities of all ability levels, emphasising participation, personal achievement and inclusion over elite competition
The Paralympics are for athletes without any disabilities, while the Special Olympics are for professional able-bodied athletes
The Special Olympics are a preliminary qualifier for the Paralympic Games and all Special Olympics athletes automatically progress to the Paralympics

explanation: Key differences: Paralympics = elite-level competition for athletes with physical, visual and intellectual disabilities, using sport-specific classification systems for fair competition. Special Olympics = participation-focused programme for athletes with intellectual disabilities of ALL ability levels, emphasising personal best, joy of participation and social inclusion rather than elite performance.
elimination_hints: A denies established differences; C reverses the disability requirement; D wrongly creates a qualifying pathway. B correctly distinguishes elite/classification (Paralympics) from inclusive/participation (Special Olympics).
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Analyze how the concept of 'barrier-free environment' or 'universal accessibility' is essential for enabling persons with disabilities to participate independently in sports facilities and educational institutions.

options.text: Barrier-free environment means removing all physical structures from public spaces so that the spaces are completely empty

A barrier-free environment involves designing buildings, sports facilities and public spaces with ramps, elevators, accessible toilets, wider doorways, tactile flooring, audio-visual signage and wheelchair-accessible seating so that persons with all types of disabilities can access and use them independently
Barrier-free environment applies only to private homes and is not relevant for sports stadiums or schools
Creating a barrier-free environment is impossible and therefore should not be attempted

explanation: A barrier-free (universally accessible) environment includes: ramps and gradual slopes (wheelchair access), elevators/lifts, accessible toilets, wider doorways and corridors, tactile paving (for visually impaired), audio-visual signage (for hearing/visual impairments), wheelchair-accessible seating in stadia, and level access to playing areas. This enables independent access and participation for all.
elimination_hints: A misinterprets 'barrier-free'; C wrongly limits to private homes; D gives up without trying. B correctly lists the design features that create genuine accessibility.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Evaluate the societal impact of successful Paralympic athletes like Devendra Jhajharia, Mariyappan Thangavelu and Avani Lekhara on changing public attitudes towards disability and sports participation in India.

options.text: Paralympic medallists have had no impact on public attitudes because disability sports receive zero media coverage in India

Their success has powerfully challenged stereotypes about disability, inspired countless differently-abled individuals to take up sports, increased media visibility and public awareness of para-sports, attracted government and corporate support for disability sports, and demonstrated that disability does not preclude excellence, achievement and national pride
Their success has negatively impacted disability perception by creating unrealistic expectations for all persons with disabilities
Paralympic success is irrelevant in India because Indian society has always been perfectly inclusive of persons with disabilities

explanation: Indian Paralympic medallists have had transformative societal impact: Devendra Jhajharia (javelin gold), Mariyappan Thangavelu (high jump gold), Avani Lekhara (shooting gold) have challenged disability stereotypes, inspired para-sports participation, increased media visibility, attracted government support (Khel Ratna, Arjuna Awards), and demonstrated that persons with disabilities can achieve excellence and bring national pride.
elimination_hints: A denies increasing media coverage; C wrongly frames inspiration as negative; D ignores persistent social challenges. B accurately captures the multi-dimensional positive impact.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Which of the following assistive devices is specifically designed to aid mobility for individuals with lower limb disabilities and is commonly provided through rehabilitation programmes in India?

options.text: Hearing aids, which amplify sound for individuals with hearing impairment
Wheelchairs, crutches, artificial limbs (prosthetics) and callipers, which support or replace lower limb function to enable mobility
Magnifying glasses, which enlarge text for individuals with low vision
Speech-generating devices, which help individuals with speech disorders communicate

explanation: Mobility assistive devices for lower limb disabilities include: wheelchairs (manual and powered), crutches (axillary and forearm), artificial limbs/prosthetics (below-knee, above-knee), callipers (leg braces for support) and walkers. These are commonly provided through rehabilitation programmes, ALIMCO and government disability assistance schemes in India.
elimination_hints: A aids hearing; C aids vision; D aids speech. Only B lists devices specifically designed for lower limb mobility.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Which of the following sports has been specifically adapted for wheelchair users and is played as a competitive event in the Paralympic Games?

options.text: Pole vault, which requires the athlete to run and vault over a high bar
Wheelchair basketball, which is played by athletes using specially designed sport wheelchairs on a standard basketball court with modified rules
High diving, which involves diving from a ten-metre platform into a pool
Steeplechase, which involves running and jumping over water barriers on a track

explanation: Wheelchair basketball is one of the most popular Paralympic sports. Athletes use specially designed lightweight sport wheelchairs with angled wheels for stability and manoeuvrability. It is played on a standard basketball court with largely the same rules as standing basketball, with adaptations for wheelchair use (e.g., a 'travel' violation involves pushing more than two wheel pushes without dribbling).
elimination_hints: A, C and D require standing, running or jumping, making them unsuitable for wheelchair adaptation. Wheelchair basketball (B) is the well-known wheelchair-adapted sport.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Analyze the concept of 'classification' in Paralympic sports and explain why it is essential for ensuring fair competition among athletes with different types and levels of disability.

options.text: Classification is simply the alphabetical arrangement of athlete names in the competition programme

Classification is a system that groups athletes into categories based on the type and degree of their functional limitation, ensuring that competition outcomes are determined by sporting skill, strategy and fitness rather than by differences in disability level — similar to weight categories in boxing
Classification means that all athletes with disabilities compete together in one category regardless of their disability type or severity
Classification is used only in Special Olympics and has no role in Paralympic competition

explanation: Paralympic classification groups athletes by functional ability so competition is fair. Athletes undergo assessment to determine their functional classification (e.g., T44 for below-knee amputees in track, S9 for swimmers with one limb affected). This ensures results reflect sporting ability, not degree of impairment — analogous to weight classes in boxing or age groups in youth sports.
elimination_hints: A trivialises classification; C would create unfair competition; D wrongly excludes Paralympics. B correctly explains the functional classification system and its fairness rationale.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Which of the following correctly describes the role of occupational therapy as a rehabilitation service that helps persons with disabilities perform activities of daily living independently?

options.text: Occupational therapy focuses only on finding jobs for disabled individuals and has nothing to do with daily living activities

Occupational therapy helps individuals develop, recover or maintain the skills needed for daily living and work activities — including self-care tasks like dressing, eating, bathing and grooming — through therapeutic exercises, adaptive techniques and modified equipment
Occupational therapy involves performing surgery to correct physical deformities in the operating theatre
Occupational therapy is identical to physiotherapy and there is no difference between the two professions

explanation: Occupational therapy (OT) focuses on enabling individuals to perform activities of daily living (ADL): self-care (dressing, eating, bathing, grooming), productive activities (work, school tasks) and leisure. OTs use therapeutic exercises, adaptive techniques (one-handed dressing methods), modified equipment (built-up spoon handles, button hooks) and environmental modifications to promote independence.
elimination_hints: A confuses OT with vocational rehab; C describes surgery; D denies the OT-physiotherapy distinction (OT focuses on daily activities, physio on movement/strength). B correctly describes OT's ADL focus.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Evaluate how inclusive education policies that integrate students with disabilities into mainstream schools alongside non-disabled peers benefit both groups of students in terms of social and personal development.

options.text: Inclusive education has no benefits and harms both groups by creating an uncomfortable learning environment for everyone

Inclusive education benefits students with disabilities through social interaction, academic stimulation and a sense of belonging, while non-disabled peers develop empathy, tolerance, understanding of diversity and cooperative skills — creating mutual benefits that prepare all students for living in a diverse society
Inclusive education only benefits students with disabilities and has absolutely no positive impact on non-disabled students
Inclusive education should be avoided because students with disabilities learn better when completely isolated from mainstream classrooms

explanation: Inclusive education creates mutual benefits: students with disabilities gain social interaction, academic stimulation, peer modelling, sense of belonging and reduced stigma. Non-disabled peers develop empathy, tolerance, appreciation of diversity, cooperation skills and a realistic understanding of disability. Both groups are prepared for functioning in a diverse, inclusive society.
elimination_hints: A claims universal harm (contradicts evidence); C ignores well-documented benefits for non-disabled peers; D promotes segregation. B correctly describes the mutual benefits.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Analyze the specific role of physiotherapy in the rehabilitation of a person who has undergone knee replacement surgery and needs to regain mobility, strength and function in the operated joint.

options.text: Physiotherapy plays no role after knee replacement; the surgery alone restores full function immediately

Physiotherapy provides structured exercises to gradually restore range of motion, rebuild quadriceps and hamstring strength, improve gait pattern, reduce swelling and pain, and progressively return the patient to functional independence — making it essential for successful surgical outcomes
Physiotherapy after knee replacement involves only bed rest for six months without any movement or exercise
Physiotherapy is only needed for patients under thirty years of age and is unnecessary for older adults

explanation: Post-knee-replacement physiotherapy is essential: early mobilisation (preventing blood clots), progressive range-of-motion exercises (restoring knee flexion/extension), strengthening exercises (quadriceps, hamstrings, calves), gait training (walking patterns), balance exercises, pain and swelling management, and progressive return to functional activities like stair climbing and daily tasks.
elimination_hints: A claims surgery alone suffices (it doesn't); C prescribes harmful prolonged immobility; D makes an unfounded age restriction. B correctly describes physiotherapy's comprehensive post-surgical role.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Match the following types of disabilities in Column A with their correct description and example in Column B.

column_b.text: Significant limitation in cognitive functioning and adaptive behaviour, affecting learning, reasoning and daily life skills — e.g., Down syndrome
Partial or complete loss of the ability to hear, affecting communication and language development — e.g., sensorineural deafness
Impairment in body structure or function affecting mobility, dexterity or physical stamina — e.g., cerebral palsy, amputation, paraplegia
Partial or complete loss of vision that cannot be corrected with standard glasses — e.g., total blindness, low vision

explanation: Physical disability → mobility/dexterity impairment, e.g., cerebral palsy, amputation (R). Visual → vision loss, e.g., blindness (S). Hearing → hearing loss, e.g., deafness (Q). Intellectual → cognitive limitation, e.g., Down syndrome (P).
elimination_hints: Physical (1) clearly relates to mobility/amputation (R); visual (2) clearly relates to blindness (S). Anchor with these.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Match the following Paralympic sports in Column A with their target disability group and a key adaptation that makes the sport accessible in Column B.

column_b.text: Athletes with severe physical disabilities (e.g., cerebral palsy) — precision target sport played from a wheelchair using a ramp if needed
Athletes with visual impairment — all players wear blackout eyeshades and locate the ball by sound (bells inside)
Athletes with spinal cord injuries — fencers are seated in wheelchairs fixed to the floor, allowing upper-body duelling
Athletes with lower limb amputations or impairments — played on a smaller court with a lower net, seated on the floor

explanation: Goalball → visual impairment, blackout eyeshades, ball with bells (Q). Boccia → severe physical disability, wheelchair, ramp assistance (P). Sitting volleyball → lower limb impairments, seated on floor, lower net (S). Wheelchair fencing → spinal cord injuries, fixed wheelchair, upper-body duelling (R).
elimination_hints: Goalball (1) is the signature visually-impaired sport with eyeshades (Q); boccia (2) is the cerebral palsy precision sport (P). Anchor with these.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Match the following barriers to disability inclusion in Column A with the type of barrier they represent in Column B.

column_a.text: Absence of ramps and elevators in a school building
Teasing and bullying of a student with a disability by classmates
A school policy that prohibits students with disabilities from participating in sports day events
A PE teacher who believes students with disabilities cannot benefit from physical activity

column_b.text: Attitudinal barrier — negative beliefs, stereotypes or prejudices held by individuals about disability
Physical/environmental barrier — structural features that prevent access for persons with mobility limitations
Institutional/policy barrier — organisational rules or regulations that systematically exclude persons with disabilities
Social barrier — peer behaviour that creates hostility, isolation or emotional harm for the disabled student

explanation: No ramps/elevators → physical/environmental barrier (Q). Bullying by classmates → social barrier (S). Exclusionary school policy → institutional/policy barrier (R). Teacher's negative belief → attitudinal barrier (P).
elimination_hints: No ramps (1) is clearly a physical/structural barrier (Q); bullying (2) is clearly a social behaviour barrier (S). Anchor with these.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Read the Assertion and Reason below and select the correct option regarding the benefits of sports participation for persons with disabilities.
assertion: Regular participation in adapted sports and physical activities significantly improves the physical health, mental well-being and social integration of persons with disabilities.
reason: Adapted sports provide cardiovascular exercise, improve muscular strength and flexibility, reduce depression and anxiety, build self-confidence, and create social interaction opportunities with peers.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but Reason is NOT the correct explanation of Assertion
Assertion is true but Reason is false
Assertion is false but Reason is true

explanation: The Assertion is true — adapted sports improve physical, mental and social outcomes. The Reason is also true and provides the specific mechanisms: cardiovascular fitness (physical), strength/flexibility (physical), reduced depression/anxiety (mental), self-confidence (psychological), and social interaction (social) — directly explaining the multi-dimensional benefits.
elimination_hints: Both statements are well-supported by research. The Reason lists the specific physical, mental and social mechanisms that explain the Assertion's claimed benefits.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Read the Assertion and Reason below and select the correct option regarding inclusive education for students with disabilities.
assertion: Inclusive education, where students with disabilities learn in mainstream classrooms, is generally considered more beneficial for the social development of disabled students than segregated special schools.
reason: Mainstream classrooms expose disabled students to a wider social environment with diverse peer interactions, real-world social challenges and natural social learning opportunities that segregated settings cannot replicate.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but Reason is NOT the correct explanation of Assertion
Assertion is true but Reason is false
Assertion is false but Reason is true

explanation: The Assertion is true — inclusive education generally provides better social development outcomes. The Reason correctly explains why: mainstream settings offer diverse peer interactions, real-world social challenges, natural modelling of social behaviours, and integration into the broader social fabric — opportunities that segregated environments with limited peer diversity cannot provide.
elimination_hints: Both statements are supported by inclusion research. The Reason explains the specific social learning mechanism (diverse peers, natural social challenges) that gives inclusive settings their advantage.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Read the Assertion and Reason below and select the correct option regarding assistive technology and rehabilitation.
assertion: Assistive technology devices such as wheelchairs, hearing aids, prosthetic limbs and screen readers play a vital role in the rehabilitation and empowerment of persons with disabilities.
reason: These devices compensate for functional limitations, enhance independence in daily activities, improve access to education and employment, and enable greater participation in social and community life.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but Reason is NOT the correct explanation of Assertion
Assertion is true but Reason is false
Assertion is false but Reason is true

explanation: The Assertion is true — assistive technology is vital for rehabilitation and empowerment. The Reason correctly explains how: devices compensate for functional limitations (wheelchair for mobility), enhance independence (prosthetics for self-care), improve access (screen readers for education), and enable participation (hearing aids for communication) — directly explaining their vital role.
elimination_hints: Both statements are factually correct and logically connected. The Reason lists the specific empowerment mechanisms (compensation, independence, access, participation) that explain the Assertion's claim.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Arrange the following milestones in India's approach to disability rights and inclusion in the correct chronological order from the earliest to the most recent.
items: I. Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act (RPwD Act) enacted, recognising 21 categories of disability
II. Persons with Disabilities Act (PWD Act) enacted, recognising 7 categories of disability
III. India's first participation in the Paralympic Games at the Seoul Paralympics
IV. India ratified the United Nations Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (UNCRPD)

options.text: III → II → IV → I
II → I → III → IV
I → IV → II → III
IV → III → I → II

explanation: Seoul Paralympics participation (III, 1988) → PWD Act with 7 categories (II, 1995) → UNCRPD ratification (IV, 2007) → RPwD Act with 21 categories (I, 2016). This shows India's progressive journey from international participation to comprehensive disability rights legislation.
elimination_hints: Seoul Paralympics (III, 1988) is the earliest; RPwD Act (I, 2016) is the most recent. Anchor with these endpoints.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Arrange the following steps in the correct order that a PE teacher should follow when planning an Adaptive Physical Education session for a student with a newly identified physical disability.
items: I. Modify activities, equipment and rules to accommodate the student's specific needs identified in the assessment
II. Assess the student's current abilities, limitations, interests and medical clearance through consultation with parents and medical professionals
III. Implement the adapted programme, observe the student's participation, and make further adjustments as needed
IV. Set individualised goals based on the assessment results that are achievable, measurable and meaningful for the student

options.text: II → IV → I → III
I → III → II → IV
III → I → IV → II
IV → II → III → I

explanation: APE planning sequence: Assess abilities and limitations (II) → Set individualised goals (IV) → Modify activities and equipment (I) → Implement and observe with further adjustments (III). Assessment must precede goal-setting, which precedes modification, which precedes implementation.
elimination_hints: Assessment (II) is always the first step; implementation with adjustments (III) is always the last. Anchor with these.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: True or False: The Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016 (RPwD Act) of India recognises 21 categories of disability, which is a significant expansion from the 7 categories recognised under the earlier Persons with Disabilities Act of 1995.

options.text: True
False

explanation: This statement is True. The RPwD Act 2016 expanded disability recognition from 7 categories (under the 1995 PWD Act) to 21 categories, including additions such as acid attack victims, dwarfism, specific learning disabilities, autism spectrum disorders, chronic neurological conditions and multiple sclerosis.
elimination_hints: This is a well-known fact about Indian disability legislation — the expansion from 7 to 21 categories is a frequently tested point in CUET PE examinations.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Study the diagram below that shows common barriers to disability inclusion categorised into four types: physical, attitudinal, social and institutional. Based on the diagram, which type of barrier is considered the most difficult to eliminate because it resides within people's minds and beliefs rather than in physical structures or written policies?

options.text: Physical barriers — because ramps and elevators are extremely expensive and technologically impossible to build
Institutional barriers — because changing a written policy document is the most complicated process in any organisation

Attitudinal barriers — because deeply held negative beliefs, stereotypes, prejudices and misconceptions about disability reside within people's minds and require sustained education, awareness and positive exposure to change, unlike physical structures or policies which can be modified through directives and budgets
Social barriers — because they only exist in rural areas and never in urban settings

explanation: Attitudinal barriers are the most difficult to eliminate because they exist within people's minds — deeply held beliefs, stereotypes and prejudices about disability are not changed by building a ramp or rewriting a policy. They require sustained awareness campaigns, positive exposure to persons with disabilities, inclusive education experiences, media representation and generational attitude shifts — a much slower and more complex process.
elimination_hints: A overstates construction difficulty; B oversimplifies policy change; D wrongly limits social barriers to rural areas. C correctly identifies attitudinal barriers as the deepest, most resistant-to-change category.
subject: Physical Education
